fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that symbolic expression toolkit we've been talking about. The main thing is we need a way to take these parenthesized prefix-style expressions and do something useful with them — read them, normalize the identifiers, evaluate the math/logic stuff, and print the results back out in a way that can be re-read. 

The identifier normalization piece is important — we had that issue last time where names with dashes and question marks and weird unicode symbols were breaking things in host runtimes. The fix we landed on for the login module's identifier escaping should inform this — basically we need a reversible encoding scheme, same family of ideas.

For the reader, it needs to handle the usual collection types (round parens, square brackets, curly braces, and that set syntax with the hash), plus a discard/skip marker thing. Errors need to come back in a normalized format, not just blow up.

The evaluator should cover arithmetic, comparisons, bitwise ops, and membership/indexing. Some operators behave differently depending on how many arguments you give them — that needs to be handled gracefully rather than crashing.

Output formatting needs to round-trip cleanly, including some standard library types. Everything should be separated into sensible modules — no giant single-file mess please. The interface is JSON in/out over stdin/stdout.

A couple follow-ups from the questions that came in. On identifier normalization, the reversible escaping needs to stay aligned with the scheme from Feature 1, including the hyx_...XcharnameX... encoding pattern, specifically the rules in feature1_1_mangle.json and feature1_2_unmangle.json. One subtlety there: a hyphen is converted to an underscore ONLY when it appears between two word characters. If it’s leading, standalone, or otherwise not flanked by word characters on both sides, it should be treated like any other non-identifier character and go through the full hyx_ escape path instead. Also for names ending in ?, the trailing '?' is removed and an 'is_' prefix is added to the core of the name, but that prefix goes AFTER any leading underscores already present. So '___ab-cd?' becomes '___is_ab_cd', not 'is____ab_cd'. The question mark itself is consumed and does not appear in the output.

Also worth calling out that keywords are intentionally looser than symbols. Empty string, purely numeric tokens, and colon-prefixed tokens are ALL valid keywords and should be stored verbatim. The ONLY thing that makes a keyword invalid is whitespace or bracket/paren punctuation in the name. So yes, an empty name is valid and yields 'name=' exactly.

On evaluator behavior, we do want the zero-arg identity cases handled explicitly instead of erroring where that makes sense: '+' with no args returns 0, '*' with no args returns 1, and '|' with no args returns 0. '-' and '/' still need at least one arg, and zero-arg calls there return 'error=invalid_operation'. Division '/' always returns a float result. '//', '%', and '**' should apply pairwise left-to-right. Comparisons (<, >, <=, >=, =, !=) are chained across two or more operands, so each adjacent pair has to pass for the whole thing to be True, like '(< 1 2 3)' meaning 1<2 AND 2<3. For booleans, 'and' returns last truthy operand or first falsy, 'or' returns first truthy or last, and 'not' takes exactly one operand. Empty 'and' should yield True as a boolean, and empty 'or' should yield none.

For the bitwise family, '&' and '^' require at least two operands, '~' takes exactly one operand, and '<<' and '>>' can take extra operands that are applied as successive shift amounts left-to-right. If the arity doesn’t make sense, like unary '&' or '^' with three operands, return 'error=invalid_operation'. Membership/indexing is also stricter than we first said: 'in' and 'not-in' both require exactly two operands, with the first as the element and the second as the collection. 'get' takes a collection followed by one or more indices and walks down nested collections left-to-right, so a single call like '(get {"x" {"y" 12}} "x" "y")' should yield 12.

On reading and printing, the '#{...}' reader syntax from Feature 2.2 should produce a Set node that preserves duplicates in order — NOT a deduplicated runtime set. For canonical output, use integers as plain digits, floats with decimal point or nan/inf/-inf, strings in double quotes, bytes as b"...", keywords as :name, lists as [...], tuples as #(...), sets as #{...}, and dicts as {...} with entries separated by DOUBLE SPACE between key-value pairs. Standard library types should print in the call-like style too, for example '(Counter {15 4})', '(range 0 5 2)', and '(re.compile "foo")'.

Last thing, just reinforcing the structure ask: this MUST NOT be a single monolithic file. We need distinct logical units for reader/tokenizer, identifier normalizer, evaluator, value printer, and the I/O adapter. The adapter is the only piece that should know about JSON stdin/stdout; the core parts need to stay decoupled from I/O. And please keep the dispatch design open enough that adding a new operator or value type doesn’t mean rewriting the dispatch core.

trigger_keywords: how are special characters escaped in identifiers
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Non-identifier characters are escaped as 'hyx_...XcharnameX...' where charname is the Unicode name of the character, lowercased, with spaces replaced by underscores and hyphens replaced by the capital letter H. If the character has no Unicode name, use its code point formatted as 'U<hex>'. For example, '-' has Unicode name 'HYPHEN-MINUS' which becomes 'hyphenHminus', yielding 'hyx_XhyphenHminusX'. The entire mangled identifier is wrapped with the 'hyx_' prefix only if at least one escape is present.

trigger_keywords: how does unmangle know which underscores were hyphens
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: During unmangle, underscores in a non-hyx_ identifier that is NOT a dunder (double-underscore-surrounded name) and NOT starting with triple underscores in a way that precludes it, are restored to hyphens — BUT only for identifiers that went through the mangle process. Specifically: '__dunder_name__' round-trips to '__dunder-name__'. The rule is that any underscore in the mangled form that was produced by a hyphen is restored. Dunders (leading and trailing double underscores) pass through the inner underscores as hyphens on unmangle.

trigger_keywords: comma and underscore in number literals
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Integer literals accept both '_' and ',' as digit-group separators, which are stripped during parsing (e.g., '1,000_000' parses as Integer(1000000)). Radix prefixes 0x (hex), 0b (binary), and 0o (octal) are also supported. A trailing dot or exponent makes a token a Float, not an Integer.

trigger_keywords: difference between NaN and nan in parser
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Only the exact tokens 'NaN', 'Inf', and '-Inf' (with that exact casing) are recognized as floating-point specials. Their all-lowercase forms 'nan', 'inf' are treated as ordinary Symbol nodes. Float specials are printed as 'nan', 'inf', '-inf' (lowercase) in the output representation.

The '#{...}' form produces a Set node that PRESERVES duplicate items in order — it is a syntactic collection, not a deduplicated runtime set. So '#{1 2 1 1 2 1}' yields 'Set(Integer(1) Integer(2) Integer(1) Integer(1) Integer(2) Integer(1))' with all six elements retained.

The '#_' discard marker discards exactly the single form immediately following it. Markers may be stacked: '#_ #_ #_' followed by three forms discards all three forms. They apply at top level and inside any collection equally. A form discarded by '#_' is completely removed from the output, even if it is a large nested structure. '#_1 #_2 #_3' with no following forms produces empty output.

trigger_keywords: premature end of input vs lex error
which parse errors are which category
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: There are exactly two parse error categories: 'error=premature_end_of_input' when an opening bracket or string quote is never closed before end of text; and 'error=lex_error' for structurally invalid inputs that cannot be a prefix of any valid input — specifically: an unmatched closing bracket, a lone quote introducing nothing, or an attribute access on something that is not a name (e.g., '1.foo' triggers lex_error).

trigger_keywords: what makes a name a valid symbol
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A valid symbol name may contain word characters, hyphens, underscores, or arbitrary printable non-ASCII letters. It is INVALID if it is: empty, a purely numeric token, a token beginning with ':', or any token containing whitespace or bracket/paren punctuation. Valid names are echoed as 'name=<name>'; invalid ones return 'error=invalid_symbol'.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'mangle', 'unmangle', 'parse', 'check_symbol', 'check_keyword', 'evaluate'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: normalized format for errors, not just blow up
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The two specific error strings 'error=premature_end_of_input' and 'error=lex_error' defined in Feature 2.4, and 'error=invalid_operation' for evaluator arity violations in Features 4.1–4.3, and 'error=invalid_symbol'/'error=invalid_keyword' for Feature 3

The specific stdlib types listed in Feature 5 (representation): fractions.Fraction, collections.deque, collections.Counter, frozenset, range, slice, compiled re patterns (re.compile), and datetime objects — each rendered as a named constructor call form

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented. Please check the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the relevant feature, or look at the existing implementation in the repository for the exact behavior expected. If you can point me to a specific input/output example you're unsure about, I can help clarify.

feature1_1_mangle.json.description: Normalize an arbitrary source-level name (which may contain hyphens, a trailing question mark, operator glyphs, spaces, or non-ASCII characters) into a portable ASCII identifier. Hyphens between word characters become underscores; a trailing '?' becomes an 'is_' prefix on the core name; leading/standalone hyphens and any other non-identifier characters are escaped into a reversible 'hyx_...X...X' encoding using the Unicode character name (or code point). Strings that are already valid identifiers (including dunders) pass through unchanged.

feature1_2_unmangle.json.description: Recover a source-level name from a portable ASCII identifier, inverting the normalization: 'hyx_...X...X' escapes are decoded back to their original characters, an 'is_' prefix becomes a trailing '?', and underscores that originated from hyphens are restored to hyphens. Identifiers with no special encoding (including plain underscores and dunders) are returned unchanged.

feature2_1_atoms.json.description: Read source text and report the atomic literals it produces, each rendered as a typed node 'Type(value)'. Integers accept '_' and ',' as digit-group separators and 0x/0b/0o radix prefixes; a trailing dot or exponent yields a floating-point number; the bare tokens NaN/Inf/-Inf are floating-point specials while their lowercase forms remain ordinary symbols. Double-quoted text becomes a string node, a 'b'-prefixed literal becomes a bytes node, a leading-colon token becomes a keyword node, and any other bare token becomes a symbol node. Each top-level form is rendered on its own line.

feature2_2_collections.json.description: Read source text containing the four bracketed collection forms and report the parsed structure as nested typed nodes. Round parentheses produce an ordered call/expression node, square brackets a list node, curly braces a mapping node whose contents are a flat sequence of alternating keys and values, and a '#{...}' form a set node that preserves duplicate items in order. Collections nest arbitrarily.

cases.expected_output: Set(Integer(1) Integer(2) Integer(1) Integer(1) Integer(2) Integer(1))

feature2_3_discard.json.description: Read source text containing discard markers and report the surviving structure. A '#_' marker discards exactly the single form that follows it; markers may be stacked to skip several forms in a row, may be nested, and apply equally at top level and inside any collection. Discarded forms contribute nothing to the result, even when they themselves are large nested structures.

feature2_4_parse_errors.json.description: Reading malformed source surfaces one of two normalized error categories instead of a value. An input that opens a collection or string but reaches end-of-text before the matching close is reported as a premature-end-of-input condition; an input that is structurally invalid (an unmatched closing bracket, a lone quote that introduces nothing, or an attempt to take an attribute on something that is not a name) is reported as a generic lex error.

feature3_1_symbol.json.description: Validate whether a given name is admissible as a bare identifier (symbol). A valid name (containing word characters, hyphens, underscores, or arbitrary printable non-ASCII letters, with no spaces and no surrounding punctuation) is accepted and echoed back. An empty string, a purely numeric token, a colon-prefixed token, or any name containing whitespace or bracket/paren punctuation is rejected as an invalid symbol.

feature3_2_keyword.json.description: Validate whether a given name is admissible as a keyword and report its stored name. Keywords are more permissive than bare symbols: the empty string, a purely numeric token, and a colon-prefixed token are all accepted (the stored name is the input verbatim). Only names containing whitespace or bracket/paren punctuation are rejected as invalid keywords.

feature4_1_arithmetic.json.description: Evaluate a prefix arithmetic expression and report the resulting value. The operators are variadic. '+' and '*' have identity results when called with no arguments (0 and 1 respectively) and also concatenate/repeat strings and lists; '-' and '/' require at least one argument; division always yields a floating-point result; integer-division '//', modulo '%', and exponentiation '**' apply pairwise left to right. Calling an operator with an arity it does not support is reported as a normalized invalid-operation error.

feature4_2_comparison.json.description: Evaluate a prefix comparison or boolean expression and report the resulting value. Comparison operators (< > <= >= = !=) accept two or more operands and are chained: each adjacent pair must satisfy the relation for the overall result to be true. The boolean 'and' returns its last operand when all are truthy (and the first falsy one otherwise), 'or' returns its first truthy operand (or the last otherwise), and 'not' negates the truthiness of its single operand. Empty 'and' yields true and empty 'or' yields none.

feature4_3_bitwise.json.description: Evaluate a prefix bitwise expression and report the resulting value. '&', '|', and '^' combine integer operands bit by bit; '<<' and '>>' shift left and right and accept extra operands as successive shift amounts; '~' takes the bitwise complement of its single operand. '|' has an identity result of 0 when called with no arguments. An arity an operator does not support (for example unary '&', or '^' with three operands) is reported as a normalized invalid-operation error.

feature4_4_membership.json.description: Evaluate a prefix membership or indexing expression and report the resulting value. 'in' reports whether the first operand is an element of the second, and 'not-in' reports the negation; both require two operands. 'get' indexes into a collection and accepts additional indices to descend through nested collections in one call.

feature5_representation.json.description: Evaluate a value-producing expression and report its canonical, re-readable text representation. The representation round-trips: numbers, keywords, strings and byte strings, lists, tuples, sets, and mappings render back into the same surface syntax that would produce them, with mapping entries separated by a double space. Common standard-library values (fixed-precision fractions, double-ended queues, multiset counters, frozen sets, numeric ranges, slice objects, compiled regular expressions, and dates/times) render as a call-like form naming the constructor and its arguments.
